description:
You are working as a configuration-management assistant.  
Your team is starting a baseline inventory of the most fundamental name-resolution facts that *must* hold true on every Linux host.  
For the very first step you have been asked to create a tiny, human-readable log that captures how the local resolver handles the special hostname “localhost”.

Goal
1. Make sure the directory “/home/user/dns_audit/” exists (create it if it does not).  
2. Inside that directory, create a text file called “/home/user/dns_audit/localhost_resolution.log”.  
3. The file must contain **exactly** the two lines shown below, in the same order, with a single space separating each column and **no** extra blank lines at the end of the file:

localhost A 127.0.0.1  
localhost AAAA ::1

4. After writing the file, display its contents to the terminal so that the change-tracking bot can capture your work.

Important format requirements (checked automatically):
• File path: /home/user/dns_audit/localhost_resolution.log  
• Permissions of the directory may be 755 (default).  
• The log must contain precisely two lines, no leading/trailing whitespace, no comments.  
• Each line has three fields: <hostname> <record_type> <ip_address>, separated by a single space character.  
• The first line must be the IPv4 (“A”) mapping, the second line the IPv6 (“AAAA”) mapping, exactly as written above.

Only 2–3 shell commands are needed (e.g., create directory, write file, show file).  
Make sure the file contents match the required format exactly; automated tests will compare the file byte-for-byte.
truth:
Expected file (byte-exact):

Path: /home/user/dns_audit/localhost_resolution.log  
Content (including newline characters, Unix LF at end of each line):

localhost A 127.0.0.1\n
localhost AAAA ::1\n
name: task_000000_07680748
